audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning a company that converts old photographs to digital format.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Picturerep.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Oh, hi.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I saw your advertisement about copying pictures to disk, and I'd like a bit more information about what you do.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, what would you like to know?
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Well, I've got a box full of old family photos that's been up in the attic for years.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Some of them must be 50 or 60 years old.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And I'd like to get them converted to digital format.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, we can do that for you.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Right, and what about size?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The photos are all sorts of sizes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Are there any restrictions?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, the maximum size of photo we can do with our normal service is 30 centimeters, and each picture must be at least 4 cm.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: That's the minimum we can cope with.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Oh, that should be fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And some of them are in a frame.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Should I take them out before I send them?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, please.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: We can't copy them otherwise, and also the photos must all be separate, they mustn't be stuck into an album.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK, that's not a problem.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, can you give me an idea of how much this will cost?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've got about 360 photos, I think.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We charge £195 for 300 to 400 photos for the basic service.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK, and does that include the disk?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, one disk, but you can get extra ones for £5 each.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, do I need to pay when I send you the photos?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, we won't need anything until we've actually copied the pictures.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Then we'll let you know how much it is, and once we've received the payment, we'll send the parcel off to you.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Is there anything else you'd like to ask about our services?
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Yes, I've roughly sorted out the photos into groups according to what they're about.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, can you keep them in those groups when you copy them?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, we'll save each group in a different folder on the disk, and if you like, you can suggest a name for each folder.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: So, I could have one called 'Grandparents' for instance?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: And do you do anything besides scan the photos?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Like, can you make any improvements?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, in the standard service, each photo is checked, and we can sometimes touch up the color a bit, or improve the contrast,
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: that can make a big difference.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK, and some of the photos are actually quite fragile.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: They won't get damaged in the process, will they?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, if any look particularly fragile, we'd do them by hand.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: We do realize how precious these old photos can be.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And another thing is we can make changes to a photo if you want,
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: so, if you want to remove an object from a photo, or maybe alter the background,
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: we can do that.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I might be interested in that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll have a look through the photos and see.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and talking of fixing photos.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've got a few that aren't properly in focus.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Can you do anything to make that better?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, I'm afraid that's one thing we can't do.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Any other information I can give you?
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Er... oh, how long will it all take?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We aim to get the copying done in 10 days.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, well, I'll get the photos packed up in a box and post them off to you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, if you've got a strong cardboard box, that's best.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: We've found that plastic ones sometimes break in the post.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, thanks for your help.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: EMPLOYEE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Updates on School Developments and Facilities

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a head teacher talking to parents of pupils about changes at the school.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, and thank you for coming here today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'd like to bring you up to date with changes in the school that will affect your children.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, the school buildings date from various times, some from the 1970s, some from the last five years, and of course, Dartfield House is over a century old.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was commissioned by a businessman, Neville Richards, and intended as his family home, but he died before it was completed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: His heir chose to sell it to the local council,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: who turned it into offices.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A later plan to convert it into a tourist information center didn't come about through lack of money, and instead it formed the nucleus of this school when it opened 40 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The school has grown as the local population has increased, and I can now give you some news about the lower school site, which is separated from the main site by a road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Planning permission has been granted for development of both sites.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The lower school will move to new buildings that will be constructed on the main site.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Developers will construct houses on the existing lower school site.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Work on the new school buildings should start within the next few months.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A more imminent change concerns the catering facilities and the canteen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The canteen is always very busy throughout the lunch period.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, it's often full to capacity, because a lot of our pupils like the food that's on offer there, but there's only one serving point.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So most pupils have to wait a considerable time to be served.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is obviously unsatisfactory, as they may have hardly finished their lunch,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: before afternoon lessons start. So we've had a new Food Hall built, and this will come into use next week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It'll have several serving areas, and I'll give you more details about those in a minute.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But one thing we ask you to do to help in the smooth running of the Food Hall,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: is to discuss with your children each morning which type of food they want to eat that day, so they can go straight to the relevant serving point.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There won't be any junk food, everything on offer will be healthy, and there's no change to the current system of paying for lunches by topping up your child's electronic payment card online.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may be wondering what will happen to the old canteen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll still have tables and chairs in there, and pupils can eat food from the Food Hall or lunch they've brought from home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Eventually, we may use part of the canteen for storage, but first, we'll see how many pupils go in there at lunchtime.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, back to the serving points in the Food Hall, which will all have side dishes, desserts, and drinks on sale, as well as main courses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One serving point we call World Adventures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This will serve a different country's cuisine each day, maybe Chinese one day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And Lebanese the next.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The menus will be planned for a week at a time, so pupils will know what's going to be available, the whole of that week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Street Life is also international, with food from three particular cultures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll ask pupils to make suggestions, so perhaps sometimes there'll be food from Thailand,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ethiopia and Mexico, and then one of them will be replaced by Jamaican food for a week or two.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The speedy Italian serving point will cater particularly for the many pupils who don't eat meat or fish.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can be sure that all the food served there is suitable for them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There'll be plenty of variety, so they shouldn't get bored with the food.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, that's all on the new Food Hall.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now after-school lessons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are very popular with pupils, particularly swimming.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, there's a waiting list for lessons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Cycling is another favorite.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I'm delighted that dozens of pupils make use of the chance to learn to ride in off-road conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It means that more and more cycle to and from school every day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, we have a well-equipped performance center, and we're going to start drama classes in there too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Pupils will be able to join in just for fun.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or work up to taking part in a play.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We hope to put on at least one a year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We already teach a number of pupils to use the sound and lighting systems in the center, and a former pupil has given a magnificent grand piano to the school.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So a few pupils will be able to learn at the school, instead of going to the local college, as many of them do at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: OK, back to the serving points in the Food Hall, which will all have side dishes, desserts, and drinks on sale, as well as main courses.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One serving point we call World Adventures.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This will serve a different country's cuisine each day, maybe Chinese one day.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And Lebanese the next.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The menus will be planned for a week at a time, so pupils will know what's going to be available, the whole of that week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Street Life is also international, with food from three particular cultures.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We'll ask pupils to make suggestions, so perhaps sometimes there'll be food from Thailand,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ethiopia and Mexico, and then one of them will be replaced by Jamaican food for a week or two.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The speedy Italian serving point will cater particularly for the many pupils who don't eat meat or fish.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They can be sure that all the food served there is suitable for them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There'll be plenty of variety, so they shouldn't get bored with the food.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, that's all on the new Food Hall.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now after-school lessons.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are very popular with pupils, particularly swimming.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, there's a waiting list for lessons.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Cycling is another favorite.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And I'm delighted that dozens of pupils make use of the chance to learn to ride in off-road conditions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It means that more and more cycle to and from school every day.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you know, we have a well-equipped performance center, and we're going to start drama classes in there too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Pupils will be able to join in just for fun.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or work up to taking part in a play.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We hope to put on at least one a year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We already teach a number of pupils to use the sound and lighting systems in the center, and a former pupil has given a magnificent grand piano to the school.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So a few pupils will be able to learn at the school, instead of going to the local college, as many of them do at the moment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

a private home.
a local council building.

items.title: What is planned with regard to the lower school?

The lower school site will be used for new homes.
Additional school buildings will be constructed on the lower school site.

dissatisfaction with the menus.

items.title: Parents are asked to

make sure their children have enough money for food.
advise their children on healthy food to eat.

items.title: What does the speaker say about the existing canteen?
options: Food will still be served there.
Only staff will have access to it.
Pupils can take their food into it.

test_text.title: Planning a Psychology Assignment on Sleep and Dreams

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two psychology students called Luke and Susie discussing their assignment on sleep and dreams.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Luke, for our next psychology assignment, we have to do something on sleep and dreams.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I've just read an article suggesting why we tend to forget most of our dreams soon after we wake up.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I mean most of my dreams aren't that interesting anyway, but what it said was that if we remembered everything, we might get mixed up about what actually happened.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: And what we dreamed, so it's a sort of protection.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I hadn't heard that idea before.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I'd always assumed that it was just that we didn't have room in our memories for all that stuff.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: What do you think about the idea that our dreams may predict the future?
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It's a belief that you get all over the world.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Yeah, lots of people have a story of it happening to them.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: But the explanation I've read is that for each dream that comes true, we have thousands that don't.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: But we don't notice those, we don't even remember them.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: We just remember the ones where something in the real world, like a view or an action happens to trigger a dream memory.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: So it's just a coincidence really.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Something else I read about is what they call segmented sleeping.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: That's a theory that hundreds of years ago, people used to get up in the middle of the night, and have a chat or something to eat.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Then go back to bed.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: So I tried it myself.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Why?
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Well, it's meant to make you more creative.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I don't know why, but I gave it up after a week,
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: it just didn't fit in with my lifestyle.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: But most preschool children have a short sleep in the day, don't they?
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: There was an experiment some students did here last term.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: To see at what age kids should stop having naps, but they didn't really find an answer.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: They spent a lot of time working out the most appropriate methodology, but the results didn't seem to show any obvious patterns.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Anyway, let's think about our assignment.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Last time, I had problems with the final stage.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Where we had to describe and justify how successful we thought we'd been.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I struggled a bit with the action plan too.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I was OK with the planning, but I got marked down for the self assessment as well.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: And I had big problems with the statistical stuff.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: That's where I really lost marks.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, shall we plan what we have to do for this assignment?
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: First we have to decide on our research question.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: So, how about 'Is there a relationship between hours of sleep and number of dreams?'
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: OK, then we need to think about who we'll do the study on.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: About 12 people.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: And shall we use other psychology students?
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Let's use people from a different department.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: What about history?
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Yes, they might have interesting dreams, or literature students.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: I don't really know any.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: OK, forget that idea.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Then we have to think about our methodology.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: So we could use observation, but that doesn't seem appropriate.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: No, it needs to be self reporting, I think.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: And we could ask them to answer questions online.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: But in this case, paper might be better, as they'll be doing it straight after they wake up,
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: in fact, while they're still half-asleep.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: And we'll have to check the ethical guidelines for this sort of research.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Mm, because our experiment involves humans, so there are special regulations.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: I had a look at those for another assignment I did.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: There's a whole section on risk assessment, and another section on making sure they aren't put under any unnecessary stress.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Let's hope they don't have any bad dreams.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Then when we've collected all our data, we have to analyze it and calculate the correlation between our two variables.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: That's time sleeping and number of dreams, and then present our results visually in a graph.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right, and the final thing is to think about our research and evaluate it.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: So that seems quite straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: LUKE

audioscript.text: Yeah, so now let's get started...
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Luke read that one reason why we often forget dreams is that
options: our memories cannot cope with too much information.
we might otherwise be confused about what is real.
we do not think they are important.

items.title: What do Luke and Susie agree about dreams predicting the future?
options: It may just be due to chance.
It only happens with certain types of event.
It happens more often than some people think.

items.title: Susie says that a study on pre-school children having a short nap in the day
options: had controversial results.
used faulty research methodology.
failed to reach any clear conclusions.

items.title: In their last assignment，both students had problems with
options: statistical analysis.
making an action plan.
self-assessment.

items: Is there a relationship between hours of sleep and number of dreams?

body.title: Assignment plan

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a sport Science student giving a presentation on the health benefits of dance.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Dancing is something that humans do when they want to have a good time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a universal response to music found in all cultures. But what's only been discovered recently,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: is that dancing not only makes us feel good, it's also extremely good for our health.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Dancing, like other forms of exercise, releases hormones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as dopamine, which make us feel relaxed and happy, and it also reduces feelings of stress or anxiety.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Dancing is also a sociable activity, which is another reason it makes us feel good.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One study compared people's enjoyment of dancing at home, in front of a video.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With dancing in a group in a studio.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The people dancing in a group reported feeling happier, whereas those dancing alone did not.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In another experiment, university researchers at York and Sheffield took a group of students and sent each of them into a lab where music was played for five minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each had to choose from three options, to sit and listen quietly to the music, to cycle on an exercise bike while they listened.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or to get up and dance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All were given cognitive tasks to perform before and after.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The result showed that those who chose to dance showed much more creativity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When doing problem solving tasks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Doctor Lovatt, at the University of Hertfordshire, believes dance could be a very useful way to help people suffering from mental health problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He thinks dance should be prescribed as therapy to help people overcome issues such as depression.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's well established that dance is a good way of encouraging adolescent girls to take exercise, but what about older people?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Studies have shown that there are enormous benefits for people in their 60s and beyond.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the great things about dance is that there are no barriers to participation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Anyone can have a go, even those whose standard of fitness is quite low.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Dance can be especially beneficial for older adults who can't run or do more intense workouts, or for those who don't want to.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One 2015 study found that even a gentle dance workout helps to promote a healthy heart.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And there's plenty of evidence which suggests that dancing lowers the risk of falls.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which could result in a broken hip for example, by helping people to improve their balance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are some less obvious benefits of dance for older people too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One thing I hadn't realized before researching this topic was that dance isn't just a physical challenge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It also requires a lot of concentration, because you need to remember different steps and routines.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For older people, this kind of activity is especially important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because it forces their brain to process things more quickly, and to retain more information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Current research also shows that dance promotes a general sense of well-being in older participants, which can last up to a week after a class.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Participants report feeling less tired, and having greater motivation to be more active, and do daily activities such as gardening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or walking to the shops or a park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ballroom or country dancing, both popular with older people, have to be done in groups.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They require collaboration, and often involve touching a dance partner.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All of which encourages interaction on the dance floor.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This helps to develop new relationships, and can reduce older people's sense of isolation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which is a huge problem in many countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I also looked at the benefits of Zumba.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: 15 million people in 180 countries now regularly take a Zumba class.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: An aerobic workout based on Latin American dance moves, John Porcari, a professor of exercise and sport science at the University of Wisconsin,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: analyzed a group of women who were Zumba regulars, and found that a class lasting 40 minutes burns about 370 calories.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is similar to moderately intense exercises like step aerobics or kickboxing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A study in the American Journal of Health Behavior showed that when women with obesity did Zumba three times a week for 16 weeks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They lost an average of 1.2 kilos, and lowered their percentage of body fat by 1%. More importantly, the women enjoyed the class so much.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That they made it a habit, and continued to attend classes at least once a week, very unusual for an aerobic exercise program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Dance is never going to compete with high intensity workouts when it comes to physical fitness gains, but its popularity is likely to keep on rising.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because it's such a fun way to keep fit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear a sport Science student giving a presentation on the health benefits of dance.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Dancing is something that humans do when they want to have a good time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's a universal response to music found in all cultures. But what's only been discovered recently,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: is that dancing not only makes us feel good, it's also extremely good for our health.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Dancing, like other forms of exercise, releases hormones.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Such as dopamine, which make us feel relaxed and happy, and it also reduces feelings of stress or anxiety.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Dancing is also a sociable activity, which is another reason it makes us feel good.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One study compared people's enjoyment of dancing at home, in front of a video.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With dancing in a group in a studio.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The people dancing in a group reported feeling happier, whereas those dancing alone did not.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In another experiment, university researchers at York and Sheffield took a group of students and sent each of them into a lab where music was played for five minutes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Each had to choose from three options, to sit and listen quietly to the music, to cycle on an exercise bike while they listened.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or to get up and dance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All were given cognitive tasks to perform before and after.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The result showed that those who chose to dance showed much more creativity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When doing problem solving tasks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Doctor Lovatt, at the University of Hertfordshire, believes dance could be a very useful way to help people suffering from mental health problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He thinks dance should be prescribed as therapy to help people overcome issues such as depression.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's well established that dance is a good way of encouraging adolescent girls to take exercise, but what about older people?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Studies have shown that there are enormous benefits for people in their 60s and beyond.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the great things about dance is that there are no barriers to participation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Anyone can have a go, even those whose standard of fitness is quite low.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Dance can be especially beneficial for older adults who can't run or do more intense workouts, or for those who don't want to.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One 2015 study found that even a gentle dance workout helps to promote a healthy heart.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And there's plenty of evidence which suggests that dancing lowers the risk of falls.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which could result in a broken hip for example, by helping people to improve their balance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are some less obvious benefits of dance for older people too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One thing I hadn't realized before researching this topic was that dance isn't just a physical challenge.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It also requires a lot of concentration, because you need to remember different steps and routines.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For older people, this kind of activity is especially important.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because it forces their brain to process things more quickly, and to retain more information.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Current research also shows that dance promotes a general sense of well-being in older participants, which can last up to a week after a class.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Participants report feeling less tired, and having greater motivation to be more active, and do daily activities such as gardening.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or walking to the shops or a park.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ballroom or country dancing, both popular with older people, have to be done in groups.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They require collaboration, and often involve touching a dance partner.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All of which encourages interaction on the dance floor.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This helps to develop new relationships, and can reduce older people's sense of isolation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which is a huge problem in many countries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I also looked at the benefits of Zumba.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: 15 million people in 180 countries now regularly take a Zumba class.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: An aerobic workout based on Latin American dance moves, John Porcari, a professor of exercise and sport science at the University of Wisconsin,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: analyzed a group of women who were Zumba regulars, and found that a class lasting 40 minutes burns about 370 calories.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is similar to moderately intense exercises like step aerobics or kickboxing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A study in the American Journal of Health Behavior showed that when women with obesity did Zumba three times a week for 16 weeks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They lost an average of 1.2 kilos, and lowered their percentage of body fat by 1%. More importantly, the women enjoyed the class so much.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That they made it a habit, and continued to attend classes at least once a week, very unusual for an aerobic exercise program.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Dance is never going to compete with high intensity workouts when it comes to physical fitness gains, but its popularity is likely to keep on rising.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because it's such a fun way to keep fit.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

Dancing with others has a more positive impact than dancing alone.
